title: New NASCAR Boss Wants to Bring Back the Fun — and the 'Badass' Feeling
summary: Do you like fast cars and loud engines? NASCAR is getting a new leader who wants to make racing fun again! Steve O'Donnell just became the CEO of NASCAR. He's the first person who isn't part of the France family to run the company. The France family started NASCAR way back in 1948. O'Donnell says NASCAR is a 'badass American sport' and that people forgot how cool it is. He wants to bring back the excitement. The old CEO, Jim France, stepped down after a big fight with some race teams. Two teams even sued NASCAR because they didn't like the new rules. They settled in December, and now teams get what they wanted. O'Donnell says he will listen to everyone — drivers, teams, and fans. He wants to make sure NASCAR is fun again. What do you think? Would you want to watch a race? Maybe you'll see some cool new changes soon!
why_it_matters: It shows that listening to others can help solve problems and make things more fun for everyone, just like on a sports team or in class.

When did the France family start NASCAR?

background_read: NASCAR stands for National Association for Stock Car Auto Racing. It holds races where cars drive very fast around oval tracks.
The France family (the family that started NASCAR) has run the organization since 1948.
Steve O'Donnell is the first leader from outside the France family.
Article_Structure: WHO: Steve O'Donnell, the new CEO of NASCAR.
WHAT: He wants to bring back excitement and fun to NASCAR.
WHERE: NASCAR, a car racing organization in the United States.
WHY: Because after a fight between the old CEO and race teams, O'Donnell wants to listen to everyone and make racing enjoyable again.

perspectives.description: Fans who love NASCAR are happy that a new leader wants to make races more fun again.
The race teams that sued NASCAR got what they wanted after the settlement, so they are likely pleased.
Some people think NASCAR races are too noisy or dangerous, and they wonder if the changes will matter.
